narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there with your middle-income family background and choice to study full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to flag high-risk cases to protect students from that tough outcome. The biggest factor pushing toward this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to most students who typically rank around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, played a strong supporting role in tipping the scales toward failure, unlike the average applicant who starts higher. Your LSAT score of 25 further reinforced this concern, as it's well below the typical score of about 37 that the model sees in stronger candidates. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, since it's closer to the average of 3.21 and shows some academic resilience. Being full-time and from a middle-income bracket offered small positive influences too, aligning with the majority of successful students. Overall, your profile stood out as riskier than typical applicants because your early and later school rankings and LSAT were significantly lower than averages, outweighing those helpful factors. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you may have faced—it's just the model's data-driven read. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas. Many students in similar situations have improved and succeeded, and with targeted effort, you can too—we're rooting for you.
